content.text: LLM API costs are charged per token — typically with separate per-million-token prices for input (the prompt and context you send) and output (the tokens the model generates). Output tokens cost 3–5× more than input tokens at most providers, because generating each output token requires a full forward pass through the model while input tokens are processed in a parallel batch. Understanding this asymmetry, and what actually drives token consumption, is the starting point for building cost-effective LLM applications.
The Cost Model: What You Pay For

attrs.alt: LLM cost model: total cost = input_tokens × price_in + output_tokens × price_out. Input includes system prompt, history, RAG context. Output includes reasoning and response content. Model tiers: small/fast (~$0.10–$1/M in), mid-tier (~$1–$5/M in), frontier (~$15–$75/M in). Prompt caching cuts input costs 90%+. Batch API gives 50% discount for async jobs.

content.text: . Both prices are quoted per million tokens ($/M). Streaming, temperature, and most sampling parameters have no effect on cost — only token counts matter.

A frontier model costs 50–300× more per token than a small/fast model. Routing even 70% of requests to a small model while using the frontier only where capability is measurably needed can cut your total bill by 10× without degrading user-facing quality.

Token counts are not character counts. English text tokenises to roughly 0.75 tokens per word (or ~4 characters per token), but code, JSON, and non-English languages tokenise differently. Always count tokens programmatically rather than estimating from character length.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Count tokens before sending to estimate cost
def estimate_cost(
    messages: list[dict],
    system: str = "",
    model: str = "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    expected_output_tokens: int = 256,
) -> dict:
    # Anthropic token counting API — does not send to inference
    token_response = client.messages.count_tokens(
        model=model,
        system=system,
        messages=messages,
    )
    input_tokens = token_response.input_tokens
    output_tokens = expected_output_tokens

    # Prices in $/M tokens (update these from provider pricing page)
    PRICES = {
        "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001":  {"in": 0.80,  "out": 4.00},
        "claude-sonnet-4-6":           {"in": 3.00,  "out": 15.00},
        # Opus prices are higher — check anthropic.com/pricing
    }
    price = PRICES.get(model, {"in": 3.0, "out": 15.0})

    cost_in  = input_tokens  * price["in"]  / 1_000_000
    cost_out = output_tokens * price["out"] / 1_000_000

    return {
        "input_tokens": input_tokens,
        "estimated_output_tokens": output_tokens,
        "estimated_cost_usd": round(cost_in + cost_out, 6),
        "model": model,
    }

messages = [
    {"role": "user", "content": "Explain quantum entanglement to a high school student."}
]
estimate = estimate_cost(messages, system="You are a science teacher.", expected_output_tokens=300)
print(estimate)
# {'input_tokens': 24, 'estimated_output_tokens': 300, 'estimated_cost_usd': 0.001219, 'model': 'claude-haiku-4-5-20251001'}
Tracking Actual Cost from API Responses
object with the actual token counts consumed. Record these to build a real-time cost tracker.

When your system prompt or a large retrieved document is sent unchanged across many requests, prompt caching lets the provider cache the KV representation on the server. The first request pays a one-time write fee (~25% of normal input price); subsequent requests read from cache at ~10% of normal input price. On a 50,000-token system prompt with 1,000 requests, caching cuts the system-prompt input cost from $125 to about $13.
2. Batch API for Offline Jobs (50% Discount)

Anthropic's Message Batches API processes requests asynchronously within a 24-hour window at 50% of the standard price. For annotation, data extraction, evaluation, or report generation where results are not needed immediately, this is the cheapest option available.
3. Model Routing: Small for Simple, Frontier for Complex

Not all tasks need a frontier model. Routing intent classification, summarisation, slot-filling, and simple Q&A to a small/fast model while reserving the frontier for tasks that demonstrably need it (complex reasoning, nuanced writing, ambiguous intent) can reduce costs by 5–20× with no user-visible quality drop.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

FAST_MODEL     = "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001"  # $0.80 in / $4 out per M
FRONTIER_MODEL = "claude-sonnet-4-6"          # $3 in / $15 out per M

# Tasks that reliably succeed on small models
SIMPLE_INTENTS = {"greeting", "faq", "summarise", "classify", "translate"}

def route_request(user_message: str) -> tuple[str, str]:
    """Returns (model, reason). Classify intent first with cheap model."""
    intent_response = client.messages.create(
        model=FAST_MODEL,
        max_tokens=16,
        system="Output the single best intent word from: greeting|faq|summarise|classify|translate|complex",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_message}],
    )
    intent = intent_response.content[0].text.strip().lower()

    if intent in SIMPLE_INTENTS:
        return FAST_MODEL, f"simple intent: {intent}"
    else:
        return FRONTIER_MODEL, f"complex intent: {intent}"

def answer(user_message: str) -> str:
    model, reason = route_request(user_message)
    response = client.messages.create(
        model=model,
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_message}],
    )
    print(f"Used {model} ({reason})")
    return response.content[0].text
4. Trim Context to Control Input Token Growth

In a multi-turn chat, the full conversation history is sent with every request. After 20 turns of 200 tokens each, your input includes 4,000 tokens of history on every call — even if the user's latest question only needs the last 2 turns. Summarise or drop old turns to prevent cost from growing unboundedly.
Output tokens cost 3–5× more than input tokens and scale with
(and the model's tendency to fill that budget). Set
to a tight limit for classification/extraction tasks (8–64 tokens) and add explicit instructions to be concise for summary tasks.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def extract_entities(text: str) -> str:
    """Entity extraction with tight output budget."""
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=64,          # entities list rarely exceeds 64 tokens
        system="""Extract named entities (people, organisations, locations) from the text.
        Output JSON: {"people": [], "orgs": [], "locations": []}. No other text.""",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": text}],
    )
    return response.content[0].text

def summarise_concisely(text: str) -> str:
    """Instruct brevity to reduce output token use."""
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=128,
        system="Summarise in 2–3 sentences. No preamble, no conclusion — just the summary.",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": text}],
    )
    return response.content[0].text
Estimate costs before launch using realistic token counts from your test suite, then apply a safety multiplier.

def project_monthly_cost(
    requests_per_day: int,
    avg_input_tokens: int,
    avg_output_tokens: int,
    price_in_per_m: float,
    price_out_per_m: float,
    safety_factor: float = 1.3,  # 30% buffer for traffic spikes and variance
) -> dict:
    daily_input  = requests_per_day * avg_input_tokens
    daily_output = requests_per_day * avg_output_tokens
    monthly_in   = daily_input  * 30
    monthly_out  = daily_output * 30

    raw_cost = (
        monthly_in  * price_in_per_m  / 1_000_000 +
        monthly_out * price_out_per_m / 1_000_000
    )

    return {
        "monthly_input_tokens_M":  round(monthly_in  / 1_000_000, 2),
        "monthly_output_tokens_M": round(monthly_out / 1_000_000, 2),
        "raw_cost_usd":            round(raw_cost, 2),
        "budgeted_cost_usd":       round(raw_cost * safety_factor, 2),
    }

# Example: 10k users/day, 800 input tokens, 300 output tokens, Haiku pricing
print(project_monthly_cost(
    requests_per_day=10_000,
    avg_input_tokens=800,
    avg_output_tokens=300,
    price_in_per_m=0.80,
    price_out_per_m=4.00,
))
# {'monthly_input_tokens_M': 240.0, 'monthly_output_tokens_M': 90.0,
#  'raw_cost_usd': 552.0, 'budgeted_cost_usd': 717.6}
Requires routing logic; misrouted complex tasks degrade quality
Cache TTL (~5 min for ephemeral); no saving if prompts vary greatly
Large stable system prompts or RAG docs
Up to 24h latency; unsuitable for interactive use
Model loses early context; summarise rather than hard-drop for quality
Truncation if output needs more space; must calibrate per task
Setting max_tokens too high for classification tasks.
A sentiment classifier needs 4 tokens. Setting max_tokens=1024 means if the model begins with a preamble you pay for all of it. Set a ceiling equal to your expected maximum output.
Using frontier models for every task by default.
The instinct to use "the best model" is costly. Test whether a small model meets your quality bar before paying frontier prices.
In a 30-turn chat, turn 30 sends 29 turns of history as input. Without trimming, costs compound quadratically across the conversation.
Using character count as a proxy for token count.
Code, URLs, and JSON tokenise differently than prose. Always use the provider's token counting API for accurate estimates.
Forgetting that prompt caching has a short TTL.
Anthropic's ephemeral cache lasts ~5 minutes. If requests arrive less frequently than that, the cache misses every time and you pay full write fees repeatedly.
